content.headline: Most dishes that taste 'off' are suffering from a simple imbalance — too much of one flavor lever and not enough of another — and tasting critically means learning to name which one.
points: Flat taste usually means needs salt; flat but with some flavor present usually means needs acid
Heavy or cloying taste means needs acid to cut through and contrast the richness
One-note taste means needs something to create contrast — sweetness, heat, or bitterness
Too sharp or biting taste usually means needs fat or a touch of sweetness to round the edges
content.tell_me_more: Professional cooks talk about 'the four levers' — salt, acid, fat, and heat (spice) — as the tools for balancing a dish. But there's a fifth that often gets forgotten: time. Some dishes taste unbalanced because the flavors haven't had time to integrate — a sauce that tastes sharp and separate after five minutes of cooking becomes cohesive after twenty. Before reaching for the salt or lemon, it's worth asking whether the dish just needs a few more minutes on the heat for everything to come together.

missions.title: Soup Balance Exercise
missions.description: Make a simple broth-based soup with vegetables and taste it critically three times — once when it's done, once after adjusting one thing, and once after adjusting a second thing. Write down what was missing each time.
missions.why_it_matters: Diagnosing flavor problems in a simple context trains the palate to identify imbalances in more complex dishes.
missions.prompt: Show the finished soup in a bowl, ready to eat. It should look golden and aromatic — not pale and thin. Show a spoon or ladle if possible. The photo should look like a bowl of soup you'd actually want to sit down with.
reflection_choices: Made two adjustments, each one clearly improved the soup — I could taste what changed
Made adjustments but it was hard to tell what each one did
Only needed one adjustment — the soup was pretty balanced to start
Over-corrected at some point and had to balance back the other direction

missions.title: Diagnosing Your Dish
missions.description: Reflect on what the soup tasted like before each adjustment and which flavor lever you reached for.
missions.why_it_matters: Articulating your diagnosis out loud — even in writing — builds the vocabulary that makes flavor troubleshooting faster and more reliable.
missions.prompt: What specifically did the soup taste like before you made any adjustments — flat, heavy, sharp, one-note? What did you add to fix it, and how did you know that was the right lever to pull rather than a different one? Did it work the way you expected?
